text: The surgeon inserts a 5mm optical trocar through an incision in the left upper quadrant, with the tip positioned behind the rectus muscle. An ultrasound is used to locate the linea semilunaris.
The surgeon observes the thin rectus muscle, guiding the depth of scope insertion through the obturator, ensuring it does not pass deeply due to the minimal muscle thickness.

The surgeon carefully advances the optical trocar through the incision in the left upper quadrant, moving slowly in real time. The visual field shows predominantly red tissue, indicating uncertainty about the correct positioning. The surgeon attempts to elevate the fat off the posterior rectus sheath, searching for the expected white tissue while adjusting the scope orientation.
The surgeon encounters difficulty elevating the fat due to numerous vessels but identifies a small window in the tissue, proceeding carefully through it.

The surgeon allows CO₂ insufflation to expand the surgical field while attempting to direct the port caudally. The visual feedback on the screen suggests a medial and cephalad direction, requiring careful alignment of the laparoscope's external portion with the intended caudal direction.
The surgeon observes the white posterior rectus sheath, indicating correct positioning. The obturator is removed to improve visibility and confirm the surgical space.

The surgeon carefully lifts the fibroareolar tissue against the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring not to damage the nerves and vessels within that plane. The space has developed well, showing clear separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon uses a spinal needle to locate the position for the second port, adjusting the scope to ensure the new port is placed close to the end of the existing five millimeter port.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the camera port, moving more cephalad to avoid a large visible neurovascular bundle. The surgeon infiltrates XPREL solution for a tap block and rectus sheath block, using a syringe to administer the mixture of Exprol, quarter percent Marcane, and saline.

The surgeon carefully dilates the port through the rectus muscle, ensuring the tip remains visible to prevent the space from collapsing. The robotic actuator is used to maintain control, avoiding penetration through the posterior sheath and peritoneum.

The surgeon inserts a Maryland instrument through the existing ports to push down on the posterior rectus sheath, maintaining the space open. In rare cases, a five-port is inserted into the abdomen to desufflate and expand the space further. If a hole is made in the posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum during initial entry, the surgeon may switch to the contralateral side.

The surgeon meticulously creates additional space for the third port, ensuring hemostasis by carefully addressing small vessels to maintain a clear surgical field. The ETEP technique is employed to prevent blood from obscuring tissue differentiation in the preperitoneal space.

The surgeon identifies a window of bare muscle to insert the third port, aiming to avoid the inferior epigastric vessels that are likely running in the fatty tissue above. The ports are spaced approximately seven to eight centimeters apart to optimize robotic dissection.
The surgeon dilates the port while inserting a spinal needle alongside it to ensure the operator tip follows the intended path. The needle is visible next to the port, confirming the trajectory.
The surgeon successfully inserts the port at the planned entry point without collapsing the space. The camera is then repositioned to the lower port to provide an upward view.

The surgeon inserts a needle just lateral to the port into the areolar tissue, performing a rectus sheath block on the contralateral side using ultrasound guidance. The surgeon injects 10 mL of XRL solution into the areolar tissue to anesthetize neurovascular bundles, while the scope from the lower port provides a view of the five-millimeter port.

The surgeon flexes the operating table to lower the patient's legs, ensuring that the robotic instruments can reach the subcostal and subxiphoid areas without colliding with the legs. The surgeon checks the tissue around the camera port to prevent smudging before preparing to dock the robot.
The surgeon inspects the camera port to ensure it is clear of surrounding tissue, confirming readiness for docking the robotic instruments.
